contest.title: 2025-2026 ICPC, NERC, Northern Eurasia Finals (Unrated, Online Mirror, ICPC Rules, Teams Preferred)

sources.notes: Could not rely on an official Codeforces editorial in this pass; tags are based on reasoning from the statement and common max-heap simulation for the game.

reference_candidates.notes: 洛谷题解候选来源；只有实际参考后才移动到 sources。

annotation.summary: Two players own arrays. On a turn, a player chooses one of their current values and attacks the opponent's maximum value; the opponent value is removed if it is not larger, otherwise it is decreased. Determine the winner under optimal play.
annotation.constraints: Values can be large and arrays have many elements, so the play should be simulated on current maxima rather than scanning arrays every turn.
annotation.core_idea: Choosing the player's current maximum is dominant: it deals at least as much damage and is not consumed by choosing it. With this observation, the game becomes deterministic. Maintain both arrays as max-heaps; each turn, compare the attacker's maximum x with the defender's maximum y, pop y if y <= x, otherwise replace it with y - x. The player who empties the opponent's heap wins. A practical implementation can batch repeated subtraction in Euclidean-style cases, but the key tagging point is the greedy max-choice plus priority-queue simulation.
annotation.complexity: O((n + m) log(n + m) log V) with Euclidean-style batching; straightforward heap simulation is the conceptual model.

solution_variants.name: main
solution_variants.summary: Reduce optimal play to repeatedly using the current maximum value and maintain both sides in max-heaps.
solution_variants.complexity: O((n + m) log(n + m) log V) with batching

tags.evidence: The main decision is proving that always attacking with the current maximum is optimal and removes all strategic branching.
The remaining deterministic game only needs repeated access to each player's current maximum.
The problem is a two-player perfect-information game, though after the greedy dominance proof it becomes deterministic simulation.
Using a larger owned value can only make the opponent's maximum smaller sooner, which justifies the dominance proof.
